title: Types of Respirators Explained: N95, Half-Face, Full-Face, PAPR, and Supplied-Air
excerpt: Learn about different types of respirators including N95 masks, half-face respirators, full-face respirators, PAPRs, and supplied-air systems for workplace safety.

children.text: Understanding the different types of respirators is crucial for workplace safety. Each respirator type offers specific protection levels and is designed for particular hazards and environments. Choosing the right respirator can mean the difference between adequate protection and serious health risks.
Quick Answer

There are two main classes of respirators: air-purifying respirators (APRs) that filter contaminated air, and supplied-air respirators (SARs) that provide clean breathing air from an external source. Within these categories are various types including N95 masks, half-face respirators, full-face respirators, and powered air-purifying respirators (PAPRs).
Two Main Classes of Respirators
1. Air-Purifying Respirators (APRs)

These respirators remove contaminants from the air you breathe by filtering, absorbing, or chemically neutralizing pollutants. They rely on the wearer's breathing to draw air through the filtration system.
2. Supplied-Air Respirators (SARs)

These provide clean breathing air from an external source such as compressed air cylinders or airline connections. They don't depend on filter cartridges and offer the highest level of respiratory protection.
Three Types of Air-Purifying Respirators
1. Disposable Filtering Facepiece Respirators (N95/N100)
These are single-use respirators that filter airborne particles. The most common is the N95 mask, which filters at least 95% of airborne particles 0.3 microns and larger.
Best for: Dust, mist, fumes, and some biological agents
Protection factor: N95 (95%), N99 (99%), N100 (99.97%)
Limitations: Not suitable for oil-based particles or gases
2. Half-Face Respirators
Reusable rubber or silicone facepieces that cover the nose and mouth. They use replaceable cartridges or filters to protect against various contaminants.
Best for: Organic vapors, acid gases, dust, and some inorganic gases
Protection factor: Typically 10-50 depending on fit and cartridges
Advantages: Reusable, various cartridge options, good peripheral vision
3. Full-Face Respirators
Similar to half-face respirators but cover the entire face, providing eye protection in addition to respiratory protection.
Best for: Environments requiring eye protection or high concentrations of irritants
Protection factor: Typically 10-50, same as half-face but with eye protection
Advantages: Protects eyes and respiratory system simultaneously
Full Comparison Table
Respirator Type | Protection Level | Best For | Limitations
N95 Mask | 95% particle filtration | Dust, biological agents | Not for oil-based particles or gases
Half-Face APR | 10-50x | Organic vapors, dust | Doesn't protect eyes
Full-Face APR | 10-50x + eye protection | Irritating vapors, dust | Bulkier than half-face
PAPR | 25-1000x | High concentrations, long-term wear | Requires battery and maintenance
Supplied-Air | Highest | Immediately dangerous atmospheres | Requires air source connection
Typical Applications
Healthcare Settings
N95 respirators for protection against infectious diseases
PAPRs for procedures generating aerosols
Construction and Manufacturing
Half-face respirators for welding fumes and organic vapors
Full-face respirators for chemical handling and spray painting
Chemical Processing
Supplied-air respirators for highly toxic chemical environments
PAPRs for extended chemical handling operations
Mining and Confined Spaces
Supplied-air systems for oxygen-deficient environments
Combination systems with escape bottles
Frequently Asked Questions
What are respirators?
Respirators are personal protective equipment devices that protect the wearer from inhaling harmful airborne substances including dust, fumes, vapors, gases, and biological agents.
What are the 3 types of air-purifying respirators?

The three types of air-purifying respirators are disposable filtering facepiece respirators (like N95 masks), half-face respirators, and full-face respirators, each offering different levels of protection and features.
What is the difference between a respirator and a mask?

Surgical masks are designed to block large droplets but don't provide respiratory protection, while respirators are certified to filter specific airborne contaminants and provide measurable protection factors.
Which type of respirator supplies fresh air?
Supplied-air respirators provide fresh breathing air from an external source such as compressed air tanks or airline connections, rather than filtering contaminated ambient air.
